title: Queues & Stacks
description: Test your knowledge of queue (FIFO) and stack (LIFO) data structures, including operations like push, pop, peek, enqueue, dequeue, and practical applications.
subject: Programming

questionText: What does FIFO stand for?
subject: Programming

content.explanation: FIFO = First In, First Out. Like a line at a store -- the first person in line is served first.

questionText: Which data structure follows the LIFO principle?
subject: Programming

content.explanation: LIFO = Last In, First Out. A stack is like a stack of plates -- you take the top (last added) plate first.

questionText: Which real-world situation is most like a QUEUE?
subject: Programming

options.text: People waiting in a checkout line
Undo button in a text editor

content.explanation: A checkout line is FIFO -- the person who arrives first gets served first. That's a queue!

questionText: Which Python module provides the deque class for efficient queues and stacks?
subject: Programming

content.explanation: from collections import deque. The deque (double-ended queue) is efficient for both queue and stack operations.

questionText: What does 'push' mean in the context of a stack?
subject: Programming

options.text: Add an item to the top of the stack
Remove an item from the top
Look at the top item without removing it
Check if the stack is empty

content.explanation: Push = add to top. Pop = remove from top. Peek = look at top without removing.

questionText: What does 'peek' mean for a stack or queue?
subject: Programming

options.text: Look at the next item without removing it

content.explanation: Peek lets you see what's next (top of stack or front of queue) without actually removing it.

questionText: Which method removes the FIRST item from a deque (for queue behavior)?
subject: Programming

content.explanation: popleft() removes from the left (front). pop() removes from the right (end). For a queue: append() + popleft().

questionText: You push these items onto a stack in order: A, B, C. What comes out first when you pop?
subject: Programming

content.explanation: Stack is LIFO. C was pushed last, so C is on top and comes out first.

questionText: You enqueue A, B, C into a queue. What comes out first when you dequeue?
subject: Programming

content.explanation: Queue is FIFO. A was enqueued first, so A comes out first.

questionText: What classic problem can be solved using a stack?
subject: Programming

content.explanation: Push opening brackets onto the stack, pop when you see a closing bracket. If the stack is empty at the end, brackets are balanced!

questionText:
What is the output?
from collections import deque
stack = deque()
stack.append(10)
stack.append(20)
stack.append(30)
print(stack.pop())
subject: Programming

content.explanation: pop() removes the last item added (LIFO). 30 was added last, so it's popped first.

questionText:
What is the output?
from collections import deque
q = deque()
q.append('X')
q.append('Y')
q.append('Z')
print(q.popleft())
subject: Programming

content.explanation: popleft() removes the first item (FIFO). X was added first, so it's dequeued first.

questionText: Why is deque preferred over a regular list for queue operations?
subject: Programming

options.text: popleft() on a deque is O(1), but pop(0) on a list is O(n)

content.explanation: Removing from the front of a list (pop(0)) requires shifting all remaining elements. Deque's popleft() is constant time.

questionText: To use a deque as a STACK, which pair of operations do you use?
subject: Programming

content.explanation: Stack: add to end (append) and remove from end (pop). Queue: add to end (append) and remove from front (popleft).

questionText: Which of these are real-world examples of a STACK? (Select all that apply)
subject: Programming

options.text: A stack of plates in a cafeteria
Waiting in a line at a theme park

content.explanation: Undo history, a stack of plates, and browser back button are LIFO. Printer queues and theme park lines are FIFO (queues).

questionText: A stack lets you access any item by its position (index), just like a list.
subject: Programming

content.explanation: With a stack you can only access the top item. You push to the top and pop from the top. No random access by index.

questionText: Calling pop() on an empty deque raises an IndexError.
subject: Programming

content.explanation: Popping from an empty deque raises IndexError. Always check len(deque) > 0 or use if deque: before popping.

questionText: A queue follows the ___ principle (abbreviation).
subject: Programming

content.explanation: FIFO = First In, First Out. The first item added is the first one removed.

questionText: To import deque, you write: from ___ import deque
subject: Programming

content.explanation: The deque class is in Python's collections module: from collections import deque.

questionText: Match each operation to its description.
subject: Programming

rightItems.text: View the next item without removing
Check if the structure has no items

content.explanation: These are the four fundamental operations. Push/Pop for stacks, Enqueue/Dequeue for queues.

questionText: Match each deque method to the data structure behavior it supports.
subject: Programming

content.explanation: Stack: add/remove from same end (right). Queue: add right, remove left. appendleft adds to front.

questionText: Put the steps in order to check if parentheses in a string are balanced using a stack.
subject: Programming

items.text: Loop through each character in the string
If it is an opening bracket, push it onto the stack
If it is a closing bracket, pop from the stack and check if they match
After the loop, check if the stack is empty (balanced) or not (unbalanced)

content.explanation: Create stack, iterate characters, push opens, pop+match closes, check empty at end. If stack is empty, balanced!
